title: Daily Wrap — Friday 22 May 2026
body: 7 meetings — Albion Park, Dubbo, Geelong, Gloucester Park, Mildura, Newcastle, and Wagga — made up the day’s 60-race harness action.

On the clock, Rocktagonal set the mark with a 1:52.0 mile rate over 1609 metres at Newcastle. For pure dominance, Aldebaran Emjay's 18-metre win at Geelong was the one. It was not the only big-margin result either, with Lou Will NZ (18m at Newcastle) and Royal Saxon (15m at Mildura) also winning decisively.

There were several training doubles to note. At Albion Park, C A Turpin prepared 4 winners; at Dubbo, J T O'shea prepared a winning pair; at Gloucester Park, G F J Bond, S A Bond struck twice; at Mildura, M L Gadsden finished with two winners; at Newcastle, G A Panella completed a training double; at Wagga, E J Stewart struck twice.

The drivers had their share of repeat winners too. At Albion Park, Pete Mcmullen produced a 4-win meeting; at Dubbo, Jett Turnbull drove 3 winners and John O'shea and Michael Jnr Day both struck twice; at Newcastle, Grace Panella finished with 3 winners; at Geelong, James Herbertson struck twice; at Gloucester Park, Aiden De Campo, Deni Roberts, and Gary Hall Jnr all struck twice; at Mildura, Jack Laugher and Luke Dunne both struck twice; at Wagga, Cameron Hart struck twice.
A first driving win was recorded by Jack Barton with Smack Bang Bart at Wagga. There was a first training win as well, with C L Barton with Smack Bang Bart at Wagga getting on the board.

Smack Bang Bart turned the market on its head at Wagga, getting home at $23. Short-priced runners also found trouble. Fire and Passion ($1.15) went under at Albion Park, Cardigan Jem ($1.28) went under at Wagga, Butter Cup ($1.35) went under at Dubbo, and Newcastle saw Next To Me NZ ($1.35) and Rocknroll Rocket ($1.50) beaten.
The race-shape picture was not uniform. Leaders were rewarded at Albion Park (7 of 11) and Gloucester Park (6 of 10).

One of the standout performances in defeat came from Cardigan Dan, who produced the day’s fastest calculated finishing burst — an estimated individual last half of 53.8 — despite finishing 2nd at Wagga.

title: Daily Wrap — Thursday 21 May 2026
body: Kilmore, Penrith, Redcliffe, and Tamworth hosted the day’s 4 meetings, combining for 36 races.

On the clock, Happy Too Ya set the mark with a 1:55.0 mile rate over 1720 metres at Penrith. For pure dominance, Grey Cadillac's 23-metre win at Penrith was the one. It was not the only big-margin result either, with Mccrikey NZ (11m at Tamworth) also winning decisively.

The trainers' column had more than one talking point. At Redcliffe, J J Hutchinson prepared 3 winners and G C Hewitt prepared a winning pair; at Penrith, K A Morris completed a training double; at Tamworth, A J Missen, L M Osborn, and M A Formosa all prepared winning pairs.

In the sulky, a few names kept coming up. At Kilmore, James Herbertson drove 3 winners; at Redcliffe, Brendan Barnes produced a 3-win meeting and Paige Bevan finished with two winners; at Penrith, Robert Morris finished with two winners; at Tamworth, Cooper Griffiths, Michael Formosa, and Tom Ison all added driving doubles.
Designer Jewel turned the market on its head at Redcliffe, getting home at $51. Short-priced runners also found trouble. Tamworth saw Nifty Studleigh ($1.70) and Arnies Army NZ ($1.85) beaten.
Leaders had mixed fortunes across the meetings. Front-runners had the better of it at Penrith (5 of 7); while it was tougher to convert from the front at Redcliffe (2 of 10).

One of the standout performances in defeat came from the Golden Hook, who produced the day’s fastest calculated finishing burst — an estimated individual last half of 56.1 — despite finishing 3rd at Kilmore.

title: Daily Wrap — Monday 4 May 2026
body: Canberra, Globe Derby Park, Marburg, and Pinjarra hosted the day’s 4 meetings, combining for 29 races.

On the clock, Applauding set the mark with a 1:56.2 mile rate over 1770 metres at Canberra. For pure dominance, Didmeselfamischief's 26-metre win at Globe Derby Park was the one. It was not the only big-margin result either, with American Eyes (20m at Pinjarra) and Applauding (15m at Canberra) also winning decisively.

There were several training doubles to note. At Canberra, N A Turnbull completed a training double; at Marburg, A J Finnis finished with two winners; at Pinjarra, D Egerton-green prepared a winning pair.

In the sulky, a few names kept coming up. At Canberra, Jett Turnbull finished with two winners; at Globe Derby Park, Wayne Hill drove a double; at Marburg, Gary Bond added a driving double; at Pinjarra, Dylan Egerton-green and Gary Hall Jnr both added driving doubles.

Sonny Rennt turned the market on its head at Globe Derby Park, getting home at $71. Short-priced runners also found trouble. Marburg saw Splashndash ($1.35), Laneys Last ($1.40), Much Bettor NZ ($1.70), and Toodalou ($1.90) beaten and Spirit of Marmyte NZ ($1.45) went under at Globe Derby Park.
From a race-shape point of view, the day had a bit of contrast. Those in front were hard to catch at Pinjarra (5 of 8); while it was tougher to convert from the front at Globe Derby Park (1 of 8).

One of the standout performances in defeat came from Vinita Rose NZ, who produced the day’s fastest calculated finishing burst — an estimated individual last half of 55.8 — despite finishing 2nd at Pinjarra.

title: Daily Wrap — Sunday 3 May 2026
body: 4 meetings — Central Wheatbelt, Forbes, Hobart, and Shepparton — made up the day’s 32-race harness action.

On the clock, Magnetic Terror NZ set the mark with a 1:54.9 mile rate over 1609 metres at Hobart. For pure dominance, Barber Shop's 20-metre win at Hobart was the one. It was not the only big-margin result either, with Taken By Wine (15m at Shepparton) and All Over It (13m at Shepparton) also winning decisively.

There were several training doubles to note. At Central Wheatbelt, G F J Bond, S A Bond prepared a winning pair; at Forbes, D L Davis completed a training double; at Hobart, H P Woods and W J Yole both struck twice.

In the sulky, a few names kept coming up. At Central Wheatbelt, Deni Roberts struck twice; at Forbes, Blake Medlyn and Jett Turnbull both struck twice; at Hobart, Grace Jones and Heath Woods both drove doubles; at Shepparton, Ryan Sanderson finished with two winners.

The biggest market surprise came via Cocosworld, who scored at $34 at Shepparton. Short-priced runners also found trouble. Imon NZ ($1.25) went under at Shepparton, Studleigh Starlite ($1.55) went under at Forbes, Alot Like Pendles ($1.70) went under at Hobart, and Sanford Harry ($1.95) went under at Central Wheatbelt.
From a race-shape point of view, the day had a bit of contrast. Front-runners had the better of it at Hobart (6 of 9) and Shepparton (5 of 8).

One of the standout performances in defeat came from Dun Spruikin, who produced the day’s fastest calculated finishing burst — an estimated individual last half of 56.6 — despite finishing 8th at Forbes.

title: Daily Wrap — Saturday 2 May 2026
body: The day’s harness action stretched across 5 meetings — Albion Park, Bunbury, Globe Derby Park, Melton, and Menangle — producing 45 races in total.

On the clock, Soho Gunslinger set the mark with a 1:48.9 mile rate over 1609 metres at Menangle. For pure dominance, Ideal An Ace's 19-metre win at Bunbury was the one. It was not the only big-margin result either, with Sea Salt (16m at Globe Derby Park) and Rockinwithattitude (13m at Menangle) also winning decisively.
The trainers' column had more than one talking point. At Globe Derby Park, J K Neilson, K Wilson completed a training double; at Melton, A A Herbertson prepared a winning pair.

The driving honours were spread across the card. At Melton, James Herbertson finished with 3 winners; at Albion Park, Angus Garrard, Layne Dwyer, and Tom Callaghan all drove doubles; at Bunbury, Emily Suvaljko finished with two winners; at Globe Derby Park, Kevin Weidenbach and Ryan Hryhorec both finished with two winners; at Menangle, Ashleigh Delosa and Cameron Hart both added driving doubles.

The biggest market surprise came via Manila Playboy, who scored at $31 at Albion Park. It was not all plain sailing for the short elects. Jilliby Ballerini ($1.20) went under at Menangle, Bunbury saw Starry Heights ($1.50) and Ruia NZ ($1.50) beaten, and Albion Park saw Oakley Shard NZ ($1.65) and Debbie Lincoln NZ ($1.70) beaten.
The maps played out differently depending on venue. Those in front were hard to catch at Menangle (7 of 11); while it was tougher to convert from the front at Melton (0 of 9).

One of the standout performances in defeat came from Can Be A Ruebe NZ, who produced the day’s fastest calculated finishing burst — an estimated individual last half of 53.2 — despite finishing 5th at Menangle.

title: Daily Wrap — Friday 1 May 2026
body: The day’s harness action stretched across 7 meetings — Albury, Gloucester Park, Launceston, Melton, Mildura, Newcastle, and Redcliffe — producing 57 races in total.

On the clock, Spunkys Gotsecrets set the mark with a 1:53.1 mile rate over 1609 metres at Newcastle. For pure dominance, Heres Herbie NZ's 23-metre win at Gloucester Park was the one. It was not the only big-margin result either, with Eye See Amour (21m at Launceston) and Black Gemstone (17m at Redcliffe) also winning decisively.

There were several training doubles to note. At Gloucester Park, G F J Bond, S A Bond prepared 3 winners; at Launceston, T R Langley prepared 3 winners; at Melton, A P Gath completed a training double; at Mildura, T H Mcguigan completed a training double; at Newcastle, K A Morris struck twice.

The driving honours were spread across the card. At Gloucester Park, Deni Roberts finished with 3 winners; at Launceston, Dylan Ford drove 3 winners; at Mildura, Jackie Barker finished with 3 winners and Jack Laugher and Wayne Hill both drove doubles; at Albury, Tasmyn Potter added a driving double; at Melton, James Herbertson and Matthew Whittle both drove doubles; at Newcastle, Brad Elder, Robert Morris, and Seaton Grima all finished with two winners; at Redcliffe, Jonah Hutchinson and Nathan Dawson both added driving doubles.
The training milestone went to A C Whittle with Moonlight Billy at Melton, marking a first winner.

Laceys Lad NZ turned the market on its head at Newcastle, getting home at $31. Favourite backers had a few bruises as well. Where It Began ($1.22) went under at Albury, King of Normandy ($1.35) went under at Melton, Newcastle saw Sugar Pie Honey ($1.40) and Rays Redemption ($1.40) beaten, and Zoe Ella ($1.50) went under at Launceston.

Leaders had mixed fortunes across the meetings. Front-runners had the better of it at Mildura (5 of 8) and Redcliffe (5 of 8); while front-runners had less control at Albury (1 of 8) and Launceston (1 of 7).

One of the standout performances in defeat came from Petes Crunch Time, who produced the day’s fastest calculated finishing burst — an estimated individual last half of 55.5 — despite finishing 3rd at Newcastle.
